coredata.dc:title: Visible light photo-treatment of simulated wastewater activated by high-efficient photocatalyst: A novel heterojunction of Bi2MoO6 balls and Pd nanoskeletons
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Huiyong
Zheng, Zemin
Liu, Mofan
Jiang, Hao
Hu, Dafeng
Zhang, Xudong

Wan, Yanfen
Yang, Peng
coredata.dc:description:
A novel Pd nano-skeleton (Pd NSs) modified Bi2MoO6 balls photocatalyst was prepared by a facile hydrothermal treatment. The characterization by multiple techniques demonstrated the excellent light absorption and effective photo-generated electron-hole pairs separation performance of the composite catalyst, which further proved its superior photocatalytic property. Compared to the pure Bi2MoO6 nanosheets, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified Bi2MoO6 balls and Pd nanoparticles (Pd NPs) modified Bi2MoO6 balls catalysts, 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 sample exhibited the optimal catalytic performance for removing methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), K2Cr2O7, phenol and the simulated wastewater mixture formed by the first three above under the illumination of visible light. Above all, the degradation efficiency increased about 5 times that of the pure Bi2MoO6 balls for the degradation of MO. This great improvement was mainly attributed to Pd NSs with many contact edges, which provided more active sites for the adsorption reaction of organic dye molecules, following its better near-infrared light absorption capacity in the catalytic system. The active species mainly involved in the reaction were verified by ESR technique and the trapping test, mainly involving
O2 −, holes,
OH, etc., and a possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism and pollutant degradation pathway were also proposed.

head.ce:title: Visible light photo-treatment of simulated wastewater activated by high-efficient photocatalyst: A novel heterojunction of Bi2MoO6 balls and Pd nanoskeletons

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A novel Pd nano-skeleton (Pd NSs) modified Bi2MoO6 balls photocatalyst was prepared by a facile hydrothermal treatment. The characterization by multiple techniques demonstrated the excellent light absorption and effective photo-generated electron-hole pairs separation performance of the composite catalyst, which further proved its superior photocatalytic property. Compared to the pure Bi2MoO6 nanosheets, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified Bi2MoO6 balls and Pd nanoparticles (Pd NPs) modified Bi2MoO6 balls catalysts, 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 sample exhibited the optimal catalytic performance for removing methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), K2Cr2O7, phenol and the simulated wastewater mixture formed by the first three above under the illumination of visible light. Above all, the degradation efficiency increased about 5 times that of the pure Bi2MoO6 balls for the degradation of MO. This great improvement was mainly attributed to Pd NSs with many contact edges, which provided more active sites for the adsorption reaction of organic dye molecules, following its better near-infrared light absorption capacity in the catalytic system. The active species mainly involved in the reaction were verified by ESR technique and the trapping test, mainly involving
O2 −, holes,
OH, etc., and a possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism and pollutant degradation pathway were also proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Faced with the trend of worsening environment, it is of great concern to explore effective channels for the crisis overcoming, which promote the development and utilization of renewable and clean energy sources [1–6]. The wastewater treatment mattering to everyone as a challenge for the environment pollution need to be tough on. In general, industrial wastewater usually contains many organic pollutants and heavy metal ions, such as the organic dye molecules like rhodamine (RhB), methyl orange (MO), methylene (MB), etc., colorless phenolic organics and heavy metal ions such as Cr (VI), Pb (II), Hg (II), etc. [7–11]. The source of pollution has great harm to the physical and mental health of the human body, mainly because of its uneasy decomposition like heavy metals, and its carcinogenicity and teratogenicity. So far, multiple methods have been taken to remove heavy metal ions and the organic molecules, such as chemical precipitation, chemisorption, stepwise separation, photocatalytic degradation, etc. [12–15]. Among them, photocatalytic degradation is considered to be an extremely effective environmental strategy [16]. The photocatalysis degradation with some kind of catalyst served as a neoteric environmental strategy efficaciously eases the pressure [17–21]. The choice of catalyst used in photocatalysis is the key to effective degradation in the catalytic process. Among the many photocatalysts, Bi2MoO6 as an unique bismuth-based material was used in various photocatalytic fields owing to the narrow band gap (2.5–2.8 eV), inexpensiveness, high stability and non-toxicity [22,23]. But the drawbacks for the Bi2MoO6 stands out, which carrying more probabilities of the recombination of electrons and holes.

In order to improve the photocatalytic activity of bismuth-based materials, many great efforts have been taken to surmount this trouble, especially for the method of coupling with different semiconductors [24–28] or diverse metal materials [29–32] to form a heterojunction expediting the charge separation and transportation. For instance, applied photocatalytic degradation of RhB all represented the preeminent result for different semiconductor heterojunctions, such as ZnTiO3/Bi2MoO6 [33], Bi2O2CO3/Bi2MoO6 [9], MoS2/Bi2MoO6 [10], CdS/Bi2MoO6 [34] and Ag3VO4/Bi2MoO6 [35], aiming at refraining from the recombination of photo-induced electrons/holes and enhancing the photocatalytic performance further. In addition, as well known, the noble metals have a large work function and can be used as good electron trapping agents. The work function of the semiconductor is generally lower than that of the metal, causing the transfer of photogenerated electrons from the semiconductor to the metal surface. So, coupling with different metals shaping variously like Ag [36–39], Au [40–41], Pt [11] and Pd [42] has been a remarkable way to elevate the optical harvesting ability based on the surface plasmon resonance effect (SPR) which is exclusively personal to the noble metals. Among many noble metal nanomaterials, Pd nanomaterials are widely used in the field of photocatalysis mainly due to: (1) The SPR absorption peak of Pd nanomaterials can be adjusted from 410 nm to 870 nm, this advantage can effectively promote the absorption of visible light and near-infrared light; (2) In the heterojunction catalyst formed by Pd nanomaterials and semiconductors, Pd nanomaterials can act as electron acceptors due to their larger work functions, so that electrons on the semiconductor conduction band can be transferred to Pd nanomaterials, this can effectively promote the separation of photo-generated carriers and further improve the photocatalytic efficiency [43–47]. However, most of the reported heterojunctions for the photocatalysis do not synchronize the absorption of full spectrum with the effective separation of photo-induced charges, which leads to the failure for the enhancement of photocatalytic performance.

Compared with traditional Pd nanomaterials, the morphology of Pd NSs prepared in this work is unique, and it mainly presents a sheet-like shape like broken lotus roots apparentlyseveredbutactuallyconnected. This morphology has more edges and exposes more crystal planes in which the defects generated can provide more active sites for the adsorption of organic molecules and the redox reaction in the catalytic reaction. In addition, it is reported that Pd nanosheets can absorb light in the near infrared region due to their SPR effect [48]. In view of this, we use Pd NSs and Bi2MoO6 materials to prepare a composite photocatalyst by a simple hydrothermal treatment to improve its photocatalytic performance. Bi2MoO6 as the base supplies more surface for the oxidation reaction. After the modification of Pd NSs on the surface of base, the synergistic effect make the catalytic performance to its extreme because of the impetus of Pd NSs owning the fascinating SPR effect, predominant NIR absorption capacity and with numerous edges as high active sites. Through the photocatalytic activity estimation based on degradation of the selected pollutants (MB, MO, phenol and K2Cr2O7) under 300 W Xenon lamp irradiation, it is indicated that the modification of Pd NSs as an effective approach can improve the photocatalytic performance. Therein, Pd NSs not only serves as NIR absorption material for the enhancement of spectrum absorption, but also as the electron reservoir to drive the electron transferred from the conduction band to the Pd NSs to participate in the reaction. Moreover, the possible photocatalytic mechanism of the Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 composite is discussed in detail.

All chemical reagents used for the synthesis are available without further purification. Bismuth(III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), palladium(II) acetylacetonate (Pd(acac)2), citric acid (CA, AR, 99.5%), tungsten hexacarbonyl (97%), ethylenediamine (>99%, GC), KBr (AR), N-Methylpyrrolidone (NMP, AR) all were purchased from Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (ShangHai). Sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), ethylene glycol (EG), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, AR, 99.5%) were bought from Macklin Inc.. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 29000) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Acetone (AR) and ethanol (AR) were fetched from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. and Tianjin Sailboat Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd., respectively. The water used in all experiments was purified by Millipore system (18.2 MΩ cm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQDs@Bi2MoO6 heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared by a typical hydrothermal treatment as follows: 1.0507 g CA and 335 μL ethylenediamine were added into 10 mL deionized water. Subsequently, the transparent solution was transferred into the 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, sustained for 5 h at 150℃. After completing the reaction until to the room temperature, the resulting product was purified by dialysis against deionized water overnight, following the freeze-drying. For the synthesis of Bi2MoO6@CQDs heterojunction, 2 wt% CQDs powder as precursor to participate in the reaction. In brief, 1.3 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.65 mmol of Na2MoO4·2H2O were dissolved into 5 mL of EG, treated by ultrasonication, respectively. Then, the two solutions above were mixed, followed by adding 30 mL ethanol into the mixture. After stirring for 30 min, a certain amount of CQDs were added, and keeping magnetic stirring for another 1 h. The resulting solution was transferred into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, treated at 160 °C for 24 h. Finally, the reaction solution was purified by centrifuging and washing with ethanol and deionized water for three times. Moreover, pure Bi2MoO6 flower-like balls were obtained by a similar method without adding CQDs and with adding moderate deionized water instead of CQDs for the product of pure Bi2MoO6 sheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Pd NSs and Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis of palladium nanoskeletons [49], 90 mg of CA, 60 mg of CTAB, 30 mg of PVP and 16 mg of palladium (II) acetylacetonate were synchronously dissolved into 10 mL of DMF by stirring for 1 h. Then the resulting solution was transferred into the 50 mL flask containing 100 mg of W (CO)6 under Ar atmosphere. Following the above, the reaction was maintained for 1 h at 80 °C under the refluxing process. Finally, the dark-hued solution was centrifuged using a sufficient amount of acetone, and then redispersed in ethanol, which is repeated three times to obtain the target product. The modification of Pd NSs on the Bi2MoO6 nanoflower balls was achieved by the similar treatment for CQDs@Bi2MoO6. Except for changing CQDs to different amounts of Pd NSs, the other processes are the same as CQDs@Bi2MoO6. The products obtained were defined as m% (wt%) Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 (m = 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.3, 1.5, 1.7, 2.0, 2.5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Pd NPs and Pd NPs@Bi2MoO6 heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Pd NPs was similar to the related report [50]. In brief, 50 mg Pd(acac)2, 160 mg PVP and 143 mg KBr were added to 12 mL NMP, subsequently leading to the mixture solution by stirring and ultrasound. The resulting solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, further conducted at 140 °C for 2 h. After cooling to the room temperature, the solution obtained was precipitated by adding sufficient acetone and centrifuged to get the product. The pure target was acquired by twice washing of ethanol-acetone mixture. The deposition of Pd NPs on Bi2MoO6 balls was similar to that of 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 sample, except that the Pd NPs was used instead of Pd NSs.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were performed with a JEM-2100 TEM (Japan Electronics Corporation) operated at 200 kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were taken with a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FEI Nova NanoSEM 450 and COXEM EM-30 PLUS) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) operated at an acceleration voltage of 10 kV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were analyzed by a Rigaku Corporation TTR18kW X-ray diffractometer using Cu radiation. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were collected on a K-alpha + photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific) using the Al Kα radiation. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) was measured by an iS10, Nicolet Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 650S). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was obtained on an Edinburgh Instruments FS5 Fluorescence Spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 363 nm. The specific surface area tests were carried out with a Uadrasorb-evo (Quantachrome, America) and analyzed by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of radicals trapped was operated on a JEOL JES-FA200 spectrometer with the spin-trap reagent DMPO in methanol and water. The total organic carbon (TOC) analysis were performed by the vario TOC (Elementar, Germany). The products of MO and MB degradation were detected by the liquid Chromatograph Mass Spectrometer (LCMS, TSQEndura).

An electrochemical workstation with three-electrode system (CHI660E, Chenhua Instruments Co. Shanghai, China) was chosen for the photoelectrochemical measurements. The saturated calomel electrode was used as the reference electrode and the Pt wire as the counter electrode. The prepare for the working electrode is taken by spin-coating 10 mg different samples on an indium-tin oxide (ITO) glass with a size of 1 cm2, respectively. The 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte was used for the photocurrent measurement. And the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in 0.1 M KCl and 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/(Fe(CN)6 4−) under a 300 W Xe lamp with 400 nm cut-off filter.

The photocatalytic activities for different samples were evaluated by the disintegration degree of various pollutants containing MO, MB, K2Cr2O7 and phenol assisted by a 300 W Xenon lamp (Perfect Light Company, Beijing) with a 400 nm cut off filter. The specific process is shown in the Scheme S1 (Supporting Information). In a nutshell, keeping a distance of about 7 cm from the reactor padded with 100 mg photocatalyst and 100 mL reaction mixture (20 mg L−1 for MO, MB and phenol, 40 mg L−1 for K2Cr2O7) vertically to maintain the incident light to radiate uniformly and employing a circulating water system to avoid the thermal catalytic effects. Moreover, a hole in the reactor need to be taken to ensure a constant supply of oxygen aiding the full reaction. Before the irradiation, the mixture of photocatalyst and reaction solution should be stirred for 30 min in the dark to reach the absorption-desorption equilibrium. For every 10-min intervals, 5 mL miscible liquids were taken to centrifuge to separate the photocatalyst, and the resulting solution was analyzed by the UV–vis spectrophotometer (T6 New Century, Beijing PERSEE) at 664 nm, 464 nm, 270 nm and 354 nm for MB, MO, phenol and K2Cr2O7, respectively. The degradation efficiency (De) was performed by the equation: De = [1 − (Ct/C0)] × 100%, where Ct means the remained concentration and the C0 is the initial concentration of different resulting solution above.

The morphologies displayed above of pure Bi2MoO6 balls and 1.3% Pd NSs@ Bi2MoO6 samples were observed by the FESEM and TEM/HRTEM. Fig. 1 shows the SEM images of pure Bi2MoO6 balls and 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6. It serves to display that the blank sample shaping the 3D hollow flower embedded by the intercrossed nanosheets with a size ranging from billionthsofa metre to 3 μm in diameter sits in more uniform distribution (Fig. 1(a–c)). Akin to that of the blank, the sample of 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 also shows the flower-like shape, except for nonuniform distribution containing some balls with different size and several bulk balls (Fig. 1(d–f)). This phenomenon confirms the result of BET test indirectly as well. For the better exploration of the distribution of Pd NSs on the Bi2MoO6 petals, the elemental mapping images was taken to verify the successful load for the Pd NSs on the blank sample. Fig. 1(g) indicates that four elements of Bi, Mo, O, Pd were homogeneously distributed in the sample. But the existence of C element maybe attribute to the organic polymer as the raw materials in the Pd NSs preparing.

Furthermore, TEM characterization was used for the demonstration of Pd NSs loading on the Bi2MoO6 resoundingly. As is showed in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), the configuration of hexagon-like, attaching the condition of the hollowness under some circumstances of apparently being severed but actually connected exports a mass of effective edges fulling of numerous defects in the Pd nanostructure, which can provide more active sites in the catalysis process (Fig. 2(b)) [49]. The lattice space of 0.233 nm for Pd NSs corresponding to the composite in Fig. 2(d) can be ascribed to the (1 1 1) plane of Pd (JCPDS- 65-2867) [49]. In the Fig. 2(c), two different lattice oriented regions containing Pd NSs and Bi2MoO6 with a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm in line with the (1 3 1) plane (Fig. 2(d)) (JCPDS-1524) [51] overlay each other, which reveals the compossibility of Pd NSs and Bi2MoO6. It’s worth nothing that no intact Pd NSs located on the Bi2MoO6 nanosheets in the TEM of heterojunctions noticeably, which can be ascribed to the situation that the Pd NSs are thinner than Bi2MoO6 nanosheets, resulting in the coverage of the thin Pd NSs by the Bi2MoO6 nanosheets with appearance of only some segmental Pd NSs pieces. Besides, the community of several elements showed by the EDS spectrum (Fig. 2(f)) is consistent with the result of the EMI in SEM (Fig. 1(g)), which all confirmed the consequence of loading successfully. Moreover, the SEM images of pure Bi2MoO6 sheets and the TEM images of CQDs and CQDs@ Bi2MoO6 sample were displayed in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information).

The crystal phases information obtained by the powder X-ray diffractometer for the different samples prepared were illustrated in Fig. 3 . Four distinct peaks showed above can be found, which is located at 28.2°, 32.6°, 46.7°, 55.5°indexed to the (1 3 1), (0 0 2), (2 0 2), (3 3 1)/(1 3 3) planes of the orthorhombic bismuth molybdate phase (JCPDS 72-1524) [51]. Otherwise, some not too obvious peaks containing what were hidden in the main maximums were well consistent with the standard Bi2MoO6, such as (0 2 0), (1 1 1), (0 6 0), (1 5 1), (2 4 0)/(0 4 2), (2 6 0)/(0 6 2), (1 9 1) and (2 6 2), which all indicated no impurity peaks existed, proving the high crystallinity of pure Bi2MoO6. Furthermore, no extra diffraction peaks emerged with increasing load of Pd NSs on the blank, which is most possibly attributed to the lower quantitative component and makes the result clear that light loading didn’t exert a significant effect on the phase structure of Bi2MoO6. In addition, the XRD patterns of the samples with Pd NSs loads of 0.5%, 1.5%, 2.5% were illuminated in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information).

XPS was carried out to explore the elemental composition and the surface chemical state of different samples above. As is showed in Fig. 4 , the presence of five elements in the composite of 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 was verified from the full survey spectrum (Fig. 4(a)) and the high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 4(b–f)). It is observed from the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4(b)) that the peak located at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, 287.6 eV are assigned to the CC sp2 hybridized carbon bond, CH bond and CO bond, respectively [52,53]. Moreover, the Bi 4f level (Fig. 4(c)) consists of two peaks at 158.8 eV, 164.1 eV, which are attributed to the Bi 4f7/2 and 4f5/2, correspondingly, confirming the Bi species exist as Bi3+ [54]. Furthermore, the high-resolution spectrum of Mo 3d (Fig. 4(d)) shows one set of peaks located at 232.0 eV, 235.1 eV ascribed to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of (+6) oxidation state of the Mo [55]. From Fig. 4(e), the peak of 529.5 eV and 531.2 eV in the O 1 s spectrum are in agreement with the framework Mo-O/Bi-O oxygen and the O-H band, separately [55]. And a set of peaks at 334.7 eV and 340.0 eV in Pd 3d spectrum is consistent with Pd 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively [49]. In addition, it is noteworthy that the peaks of three elements (Bi, Mo, O) all exhibit obvious shifts compared with the blank sample. This reveals that there are some interaction between the Pd NSs and the Bi2MoO6.

The FT-IR spectra for different samples above are displayed in Fig. S3. As can be seen from the spectra, there are about six characteristic peaks located at 448 cm−1, 584 cm−1, 728 cm−1, 794 cm−1, 838 cm−1 and 1648 cm−1 ranging from 400 cm−1 to 2000 cm−1 for the pure Bi2MoO6, which can be in keep with the variation of crystal structure of the blank [56]. In particular, the band sited at 448 cm−1 is ascribed to the Bi-O stretching mode, while the peaks of 584 cm−1 and 728 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration and asymmetric stretching modes of MoO6, respectively. The absorption band at 794 cm−1 and 838 cm−1 can be matched with the asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes of MoO6 accompanied by the vibrations of the apical oxygen atoms [56]. Moreover, some puny peaks from 1200 cm−1 to 1700 cm−1 may be concerned with the absorbed CH3COOH involving the δCH vibration of the CH3CO moiety and the coupled υCO, δOH vibrations [57,58]. For the composites, no distinct variations are found with increasing load of Pd NSs on the Bi2MoO6, which indicates that the modification of Pd NSs on the Bi2MoO6 did not change the structure of Bi2MoO6.

The optical properties of pure Bi2MoO6 and the m% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 composites were performed by the UV–Vis DRS. As showed in Fig. 5 (a), the absorption edge of the blank sample is about 475 nm, corresponding to the band gap energy (Eg) about 2.61 eV obtained from the Kubelka-Munk equation [54]. With the weight gaining in m value for the composite, the absorption edge red shift to the maximum at 1.3% about 574 nm, followed by the decline from 1.3% to 2.0%. This can be ascribed to the synergetic effect of heterojunction between Bi2MoO6 and the Pd NSs with the SPR. Based on the Kubelka-Munk equation, combining with the empirical equation EVB = X − Ee + 1/2Eg (X is the electronegativity of semiconductor, Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV)) [59], the EVB of pure Bi2MoO6 is calculated to be 2.36 eV, close to the 2.42 eV measured by the XPS from the Fig. 5(b). This result also can be confirmed by the similar report [52]. After loading the Pd NSs, the Eg of formed composite is shrunk to about 2.16 eV, which can be applied to the formula ECB = EVB − Eg, making the ECB gained ~0.02 eV for the heterojunction, about −0.19 eV for the blank sample in the similar way. The results above indicates that the formed heterojunction with a smaller band gap effectively promotes the absorption of visible light and the improvement of the photocatalytic ability.

The evaluation for the recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs was carried out by the PL spectra. In Fig. 6 (a), the pure Bi2MoO6 balls without Pd NSs loading exerts higher recombination rate compared with the composites loading Pd NSs weighting from 0.3% to 2%, especially for the 1.3% load, showing the optimum for the resistance of photo-generated electrons and holes to combine. What has been discussed above adequately indicates that the load of Pd NSs on the Bi2MoO6 is in favor of inhibiting the recombination of photo-excited carriers, leading to the enhancement of the amount of photogenic electrons and holes and the improvement of photocatalytic activity under the visible light. Moreover, the PL intensity of composites with the load below 1.3% drops gradually, and then slightly rises as the Pd NSs content continues to increase, which illuminates that too high Pd NSs loading level may generate more recombination centers used for the capture of photogenic electrons and holes, resulting in the ascent of recombination rate again [60]. Furthermore, to understand the photophysical behaviors of carriers, the time-resolved transient PL decay are performed for the blank and composite samples (Fig. 6(b)). According to the corresponding lifetime calculation formula [61]:

It can be calculated the lifetime of photogenerated charge carriers for the 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 sample and the blank sample are 1.3404 ns and 1.0400 ns, respectively, as shown in Fig. S4. Apparently, the 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 displays a longer PL lifetime than the blank, which indicates that Pd NSs modification effectively promotes the separation of photo-exited electron-hole pairs, transferring from the Bi2MoO6 to Pd NSs [62].

For a deep investigation in the photophysical behaviors of photo-generated electrons and holes, the transient photocurrent and the electrochemical impedance tests for the blank and 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 samples are recorded in Fig. 7 . From the Fig. 7(a), it was found that the transient photocurrent response of composite with 1.3% Pd NSs loading was higher than that of the blank. This result is associated with that analyzed by the PL spectra and the time-resolved transient PL decay spectra, suggesting a more efficient inhibition of recombination and the longer lifetimes of photo-exited carriers. Furthermore, the EIS spectra in Fig. 7(b) verifies the result above through the comparison in the impedance arc radius for pure Bi2MoO6 balls and 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6, where the smaller radius exists for the 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 than the blank sample, indicating an enhanced separation efficiency of photogenic electron-hole pairs in the composite. In this regard, the results based on the two analysis reveals an analogous trend in connection with the photocatalytic activity [63].

As well known, the specific surface area of photocatalysts is related to the amount of absorbed active species, the bigger specific surface area, the more adequate photocatalysts is in contact with the contaminant molecules, leading to the enhanced photocatalytic activity [52]. The results of different samples prepared was illuminated in Fig. S5. Calculated by means of characterization, the specific surface area of the 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 is the maximum (83.070 m2/g) compared with that of the pure Bi2MoO6 balls (58.115 m2/g). And the trend goes up gradually below the weight loading of 1.3%, then decreases tardily over the 1.3%. Obviously, This large specific surface area may produce a positive effect on the photocatalytic property [64].

Actuated by the motive of the effectively judging photocatalytic performance of the prepared catalyst, MO (20 mg/L), MB (20 mg/L), phenol (20 mg/L) and K2Cr2O7 (40 mg/L) were selected as the pollution source for the photocatalytic degradation. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), after stirring for 30 min in a dark environment, the MO concentration shows a slight decrease without substantial effect. And the degradation efficiency of pure Bi2MoO6 sheets and Bi2MoO6 balls maintains lower, just about 10% to 20% after 80 min of illumination. When the CQDs is loaded onto the Bi2MoO6 balls, the efficiency is significantly improved, but only close to 50%. As the loading of Pd NSs on the Bi2MoO6 balls increases, the degradation efficiency increases gradually as well, reaching a maximum of 95% at 1.3% loading, which is about 4.75 times than that of the blank sample. After the loading exceeds 1.3%, the catalytic effect begins to decline. This phenomenon is consistent with the results of PL characterization, indicating that excess Pd NSs loading will produce more recombination centers to capture photogenerated electrons and holes. The catalytic effect of the heterojunction catalyst formed by the above Pd NSs supported Bi2MoO6 balls is significantly higher than that of the CQD loading, mainly due to the fact that Pd NSs have more contact edges in the catalytic process, which provides abundant active sites for the adsorption process of organic molecules in pollutants. Besides, it also proves that the loading of Pd NSs is an extremely effective channel in separating photogenerated electrons and holes. Fig. 8(b) shows the kinetic fit corresponding to the catalytic process of different catalysts in (a), based on the formula −ln(Ct/C0) = kt [65]. It can be found that the slope of the 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 sample is the largest (0.03356 min−1), which is much higher than the blank sample (0.00327 min−1), consistent with the photocatalytic activity test results. Furthermore, to realize the functional diversity of the catalyst prepared, the 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 sample was utilized for the degradation of MB and K2Cr2O7. It can be seen from Fig. 8(c–f) that the optimal loading of Pd NSs is effective for the degradation of MB and the reduction of Cr (VI). After the 80-minute lighting, the degradation efficiency of MB is almost 100%, and about 80% for the high-concentration K2Cr2O7 which is not easily degraded under 120 min of illumination. The results above also can be obtained more intuitively from the absorption spectrum in Fig. 8(g–j), and fully demonstrate the superior photocatalytic performance of the catalyst prepared in this work. Moreover, the MO photocatalytic degradation efficiencies and the kinetic fit of the samples (m = 0.5, 1.5, 2.5) were presented in Fig. S6, and the temporal UV–vis absorption spectral changes of MO corresponding to the samples above were exhibited in Fig. S7 (Supporting Information). Furthermore, the TOC analysis was performed for the MO and MB solutions at different times to further evaluate the mineralization rates of MO and MB under visible light. Fig. S8 shows that the mineralization rate increases with the reaction time, and the mineralization rates of MO and MB can reach 59.6% and 70.5% at 80 min, respectively. This indicates that most of the organic carbon in MO and MB decomposes into inorganic carbon (CO2) after long-term photodegradation. At the same time, it can be found that the catalyst used in this work exhibits excellent catalytic performance by comparison with related literature reports (Table S1, S2, S3).

In addition, we successfully prepared Pd NPs, and supported 1.3% Pd NPs on Bi2MoO6 to form the 1.3% Pd NPs@Bi2MoO6 heterojunction catalyst. The TEM characterization proved that Pd NPs were successfully loaded on Bi2MoO6, as shown in Fig. S9. The photocatalytic performances of pure Bi2MoO6 balls, 2.0% CQDs@Bi2MoO6, 1.3% Pd NPs@Bi2MoO6 and 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts were compared by the degradation of MO, MB, Cr (VI) and phenol from Figs. S10-S13. By comparison, it is found that the photocatalytic activity of 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst is better than that of the 1.3% Pd NPs@Bi2MoO6 and 2.0%CQDs@Bi2MoO6 samples.

For the sake of simulation of the wastewater environment, three pollution sources, MO, MB and K2Cr2O7, were mixed for the further photocatalytic degradation. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), in the presence of pure Bi2MoO6 balls catalyst, all three pollutants have shown good degradation effects. It can be seen that the characteristic peaks of K2Cr2O7 and MO have disappeared, only the obvious characteristic of MB. Compared with the 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 composite, the overall catalytic effect is higher than that of the blank sample (85.2%), remaining 96.8% for the degradation efficiency (Fig. 9(b)). This indicates that there is a synergistic effect with the simultaneous existence of the oxidation of the organic dye and the reduction of Cr (VI) in the photocatalytic reaction [66]. However, it is worth noting that after stirring the reactants and catalyst for 30 min in a dark environment, the characteristic peak of MB almost disappeared abruptly. As the illumination time prolonged, the peak intensity gradually increased again and then decreased. In addition, the characteristic peak of MO also drops sharply with the lighting time expansion.

In order to explore the reasons, the above results were explained by the additional experiments with exclusion methods. At first, the catalyst is removed, following the above catalytic process. As illuminated in Fig. 9(c), after the dark stirring for 30 min without participation of the catalyst, there is no sudden disappearance of the above-mentioned MB characteristic peak, indicating that the presence of the catalyst results in the disappearance of MB characteristic peak, and it was also found that the peaks of MO still drop sharply as before. Therefore, further exclusion experiments were carried out in which MO and MB were mixed, MO and K2Cr2O7 were mixed for the same catalytic process in the absence of catalyst. As revealed in Fig. 9(d) and (e), the characteristic peak of MO does not change much from beginning to end in the presence of only MO and K2Cr2O7, but for the mixture of MO and MB, the peak of MO appears with a sharp decline, which fully indicates that the MO will react with the MB under the visible light, leading to the gradual decrease of MO. Excluded by the above experiment, the possible reaction process is as follows: MB with strong oxidizing property oxidizes MO under the visible light with the aid of catalyst, resulting in rapid disappearance of MO, thereby making MB become the reducible methylene white. In the presence of oxidative K2Cr2O7, the reductive methylene white is further oxidized to MB by K2Cr2O7, which leads to the gradual increase of MB, and is gradually decomposed. During the catalytic process of K2Cr2O7, Cr (VI) is reduced to Cr (III), and the MB oxidized by K2Cr2O7 again is also degraded gradually in the photocatalytic process. Besides, due to the above synergistic effects, the reduction process of Cr (VI) is accelerated.

To further explore the inherent mechanism in the photocatalytic process, the trapping tests were carried out to verify whether the reactive species play a role in the course or not. The isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (p-BZQ) and ammonium oxalate (AO) as the scavengers were used to track the states of
OH,
O2 − and h+ in the degradation of pollutants, respectively. As demonstrated in the Fig. 10 (a–b), the major decline of degradation rate is rooting in the existence of the scavenger p-BZQ (24.222%) and AO (24.705%) acting on the
O2 − and h+, making clear that
O2 − and h+ play the vital role during the photo-degradation in this system. It’s worth nothing that no distinct changes for the products after adding the IPA reacting on the
OH, the degradation rate just reduces by 8.71% compared with the no scavenger, indicating that the function of
OH is less obvious in the process of degradation. The result discussed above can be exhibited intuitively in the Fig. 10(c–e) as well. Otherwise, the much better photocatalytic performance for the 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 sample than the pure Bi2MoO6 balls verifies the excellent redox ability for this novel heterojunction.

The spin-trapping ESR of pure Bi2MoO6 and 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 samples in different conditions was taken for the intensive study about the photocatalytic mechanism. As exhibited in Fig. 11 (a), ESR signals are next to nothing when no light performed in the two system above, which indicates that no
OH generate. While lighting 5 min and 10 min later, the ESR signals of DMPO-
OH were enhanced sharply for the composite sample compared with the blank sample. This can be ascribed to the high concentration of
OH rooting in the aqueous dispersion oxidized by the photogenerated holes, which also confirms the enhanced isolation efficiency of photogenerated electron and holes further through the hybridization process [67]. By the same token, Fig. 11(b) displays the DMPO-
O2 − ESR signals distribution of pure Bi2MoO6 and 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 samples in disparate cases. The signal strength of the adduct heightens with prolonging the lighting time in the two systems above, which represents the concentration grade of
O2 −. But in contrast to the pure Bi2MoO6 balls, the composite sample shows higher
O2 − formation rates, masterly revealing the responsibility of Pd NSs in the formation of superoxide radicals and the course of photocatalysis, which implies the composite augments the yield of
O2 −, where the oxidizing ability results in a higher pollutants degradation rate.

To further evaluate the stability of the prepared catalyst during catalytic degradation, the cycling test was adopted for the 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 composite toward the MO degradation. As displayed in Fig. 12 (a), after five cycles, the photocatalytic activity of 1.3% Pd NSs@Bi2MoO6 maintains without a significant decrease and the XRD patterns remains stable and have little changes (Fig. 12(b)). The above results all indicate that the catalyst in this system shows a high stability and reusability, which fully comply with the sustainable development strategy guidelines. Furthermore, the same photocatalytic tests on MO, MB, and Cr (VI) for five times were performed to study the stability of photocatalytic reaction. It can be seen from the Fig. S14 that the photocatalytic reaction for three pollutants showed good stability under the same test conditions.

Based on the results above, the probable mechanism in this system is illustrated in Fig. 13 . With the irradiation from the sunlight, the excited e− in Bi2MoO6 under the visible light transferred from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), following on jumping to Pd NSs serving as charge acceptor to accelerate the separation of e−/h+ pairs. As reported in relevant literatures [68,69], the work function of Pd is 5.12 V at vacuum (0.62 eV vs. HNE). For n-type semiconductors, the flat band potential is equal to the Fermi level [70], which is also equal to its work function, and usually n-type the Fermi level of a semiconductor is close to its conduction band potential (0.1–0.2 eV more positive) [71]. According to the calculated work function of Bi2MoO6 reported in related literature, the work function is approximately −0.131 eV vs. Ag/AgCl (0.069 eV vs. NHE) [72], so the work function of Pd is greater than that of Bi2MoO6, leading to the formation of a Schottky barrier. Thereby, Pd can serve as an electron acceptor to receive the electrons on the Bi2MoO6 conduction band and the SPR-induced hot electrons transferred to the Bi2MoO6 conduction band to promote the separation of photogenerated carriers. So, due to its SPR effect, Pd NSs can be activated by light radiation in the wavelength range of 410–870 nm. When the excited hot electron has a potential that is more negative than the conduction band potential of Bi2MoO6, it may transfer to Bi2MoO6′s conduction band, thus enhancing its photocatalytic activity [43,46,68]. As a result, the oxidation reaction takes place on the VB of Bi2MoO6 by reacting with h+ following a product of
OH obtained, ascribing to the more positive position of the VB of Bi2MoO6 (2.42 eV vs. NHE) than the Eo(
OH/OH−)(2.38 eV vs. NHE), and the redox reaction happens on the edges of Pd NSs as high active sites and the CB of Bi2MoO6 by the e− adsorption for the purpose of
O2 −, resulting from the less positive location of CB potential (-0.19 eV) than Eo(O2/
O2 −)(-0.046 eV vs. NHE), which both are used to realize the degradation for different pollutants finally. The overall photocatalytic reaction mechanism is as follows:
(1)
Light + Bi2MoO6 → e− + h+
(2)
NIR + Pd NSs → e−
(3)
e−(CB of Bi2MoO6) → Pd NSs
(4)
O2 + e−(Pd NSs edges and CB of Bi2MoO6) →
O2 −; Cr (VI) + e− → Cr (III)
(5)
H2O + h+(VB of Bi2MoO6) →
OH
(6)
Pollutants + h+ +
OH +
O2 − → CO2 + H2O

Combined with ESR spectral analysis and the possible degradation pathway in photo-degradation process for the MO and MB pollutants detected by LCMS (Figs. S15 and S16), all the results indicate that the
O2 − and holes play a key role in the catalytic degradation process. In addition, hydroxyl radicals are also indispensable. Several reactive oxidative species act together for the degradation of the pollutants in the photocatalytic process.

In summary, a novel Pd NSs modified Bi2MoO6 catalyst was successfully synthesized by a facial hydrothermal treatment. The formed heterojunction catalyst exhibited a significant photocatalytic activity in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants MO, MB, phenol and the reduction of Cr (VI). Saving the synergistic effect of simultaneous reduction of Cr (VI) and oxidation of organic dyes, the catalysts used in the system also performed well in the treatment of simulated wastewater. This significant improvement for the photocatalytic performance is mainly attributed to the crucial role of Pd NSs, including more active sites, superior SPR effects, stronger near-infrared absorption properties, and effective inhibition of photo-generated carrier recombination. The main active species during the reaction were determined by the
O2 − and h+, following the
OH involved. Based on the analysis of experimental results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. This work further will provide ideas for the new design of noble metal/semiconductor catalysts.
